journal_entry.description: Client entertainment meal paid by corporate credit card
journal_entry.rationale: The $125 total includes HST. In Ontario (assuming HST 13%), we reverse-calculate: net meal = $125 / 1.13 = $110.62, HST = $14.38. Meals and Entertainment Expense is debited for the net amount, HST Receivable is debited for the recoverable tax (note: only 50% of meals and entertainment expense is deductible for income tax purposes, but 100% of HST is recoverable as an input tax credit), and Credit Card Payable is credited for the total amount charged.

output.reason: The $125 includes HST that must be separated. Debit side has TWO lines: (1) Meals & Entertainment expense increase (net amount before tax), and (2) HST Recoverable/Input Tax Credit asset increase (the recoverable tax portion). This gives us 1 asset increase + 1 expense increase.

debit_classifier.reason: The $125 includes HST that must be separated. Debit side has TWO lines: (1) Meals & Entertainment expense increase (net amount before tax), and (2) HST Recoverable/Input Tax Credit asset increase (the recoverable tax portion). This gives us 1 asset increase + 1 expense increase.

output.reason: Corporate credit card used = liability increase (accounts payable/credit card payable increases when the card is used)

Meals and Entertainment Expense is debited for the net amount, HST Receivable is debited for the recoverable tax (note: only 50% of meals and entertainment expense is deductible for income tax purposes, but 100% of HST is recoverable as an input tax credit), and Credit Card Payable is credited for the total amount charged.

output.reason: Entry correctly records client entertainment meal with HST separation. Balance verified: debits ($110.62 + $14.38 = $125.00) equal credits ($125.00). HST calculation correct: $125 / 1.13 = $110.62 net, $14.38 HST. Accounts appropriate: Meals and Entertainment Expense for net amount, HST Receivable for input tax credit (100% recoverable regardless of 50% income tax limitation), Credit Card Payable for liability. All amounts reasonable and complete.

rejection_reason: Missing tax lines. In Canada, restaurant meals are subject to GST/HST which must be separately recorded. The $125 total includes tax that should be broken out: the expense should be recorded net of tax, with separate HST Recoverable/Input Tax Credit and tax expense components. The generator incorrectly assumed tax is simply included without separate accounting treatment.

output_debit_classifier.reason: Client entertainment meal = expense increase. The $125 meal is a business expense (entertainment/meals and entertainment expense). The debit increases the expense account. The credit side (not classified here) would increase the liability (corporate credit card payable).

output_entry_builder.description: Client entertainment meal paid with corporate credit card
output_entry_builder.rationale: Under IFRS, meals for client entertainment are recorded as Meals and Entertainment Expense (operating expense). The corporate credit card creates a liability (Credit Card Payable). In Canada, meals are subject to 50% deductibility for tax purposes, but the full amount is recorded as expense with the limitation applied at tax filing. The transaction states the business purpose clearly (client entertainment), so no ambiguity exists. No HST/GST is added because restaurant meals typically have tax included in the stated price, and the transaction amount of $125 appears to be the total paid.

fix_plans.fix_context: The $125 includes HST that must be separated. Debit should include: expense increase (net meal cost) AND asset increase (HST Recoverable/Input Tax Credit). Count both categories.
